FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: 7,445 adolescents (3,511 females, 3,934 males) aged 9-10 years at baseline (2016-2018) with 2-year follow-up. Prospective longitudinal design explicitly stated.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as aggregate composite across all modalities (TV, movies, video games, socializing/texting/video chatting, social media) without separately extractable smartphone or social media-specific IV coefficient in primary analyses. Main text and Table 2 report only aggregate screen time coefficient (β=0.32 for BMI, β=0.40 for WC). Supplementary tables mentioned for modality-specific associations, but E2 rule applies: whole-composite only = NOT_MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; exclusion applies via cascade rule.

FT_IC4.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; exclusion applies via cascade rule.

FT_IC5.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; exclusion applies via cascade rule.

FT_IC6.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; exclusion applies via cascade rule.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; does not reach FT-EC7 evaluation.
notes: Disagreement resolver decision: Paper presents aggregate screen time (generic STS composite) without separately reported smartphone/SM-specific IV coefficient in primary analyses. Although supplementary tables likely contain modality breakdown, the main paper fails FT-IC2 per E2 rule. C1 correctly identified NOT_MET; C2 misapplied the exposure criterion by counting the existence of type categories as equivalent to separately extractable coefficients for smartphone/SM variables in the primary analysis.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: abstract explicitly states 'Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study were analyzed.' U.S. ABCD confirmed by context.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as specific variable with modality breakdown: weekday evenings and weekend evenings measured separately. Paper reports screen time as distinct predictor in SEM analyses. Text: 'Screen time was more prevalent among Hispanic adolescents, particularly on weekday evenings (Cohen's d=0.27) and weekend evenings (Cohen's d=0.35).' Screen time enters primary SEM model as separate IV.

FT_IC3.evidence: SEM and t-tests reported: screen time associated with chronotype, social jet lag, and weekday sleep duration. Text: 'Screen time...was strongly associated with later chronotype and greater social jet lag.' SEM model with screen time as IV predicting sleep/chronotype outcomes. Multiple inferential tests present.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric effect sizes reported: Cohen's d for screen time on weekday evenings (d=0.27) and weekend evenings (d=0.35). SEM model variance explained: 12.9% for chronotype, 10.5% for social jet lag, 6.2% for weekday sleep duration.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional and SEM examining behavioral, social, and psychological predictors of chronotype and sleep outcomes in ABCD adolescents. Structural equation modeling with multiple measured variables as IVs.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Sleep Advances: A Journal of the Sleep Research Society, 2025. Peer-reviewed, not a preprint or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is an independent variable (predictor), not the dependent variable. Paper examines associations between screen time and chronotype/sleep outcomes. Additionally, paper includes individual psychological predictors (perceived stress, depression, anxiety) alongside demographics, which per rule (b) and criterion excludes FT-EC7 application.
notes: Both C1 and C2 agree on all criteria and FT-EC7 determination. Agreement confirmed by resolver.
